planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to part shade; Ceylon gooseberry grows rapidly and begins fruiting in 3–4 years from seed; it is dioecious (separate male and female plants needed for fruit), so plant at least one male for every 3–5 females.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; plant both male and female trees for fruit production. Dovyalis hebecarpa (Ceylon gooseberry or kitambilla) is a Sri Lankan thorny shrub or small tree producing small, 1-to-1.5-inch, velvety, dark-purple to maroon fruits with an intensely tart, acidic flavor somewhat resembling a sour cranberry or gooseberry crossed with tamarind — extremely sour fresh but outstanding for jams, jellies, preserves, and syrups when sweetened. The plant is densely armed with sharp thorns and can be grown as an impenetrable security hedge while also producing edible fruit — a combination making it practically useful as a barrier plant in tropical gardens. In Sri Lanka, the fruits are made into a traditional preserve and the juice is used as a refreshing tart drink. The tree tolerates moderate drought and poor soils once established and can be used as a rootstock for grafting related Dovyalis species. It is dioecious — plant both male and female plants for fruiting. The compact form and spiny character make it useful as a living fence.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Fruits
Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Dovyalis

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after last frost in well-drained, moist, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; ridged luffa is faster-maturing and milder-flavored than smooth luffa and is the preferred luffa for eating — it should be harvested at 6–8 inches when young and tender, as it becomes bitter and fibrous if allowed to mature.

directions: Direct sow in warm soil in well-drained, moist, moderately fertile soil in full sun; provide strong trellis — ridged luffa is a vigorous climber. Luffa acutangula (ridged luffa or angled loofah) is the more tender, edible species of luffa — a tropical Asian cucurbit vine grown throughout South and Southeast Asia as a primary vegetable crop. Ridged luffa is distinguished from smooth luffa (L. aegyptiaca) by its deeply ridged, angled, dark-green fruits (hence 'ridged' and 'angled' in the name) and by being significantly milder, more tender, and less bitter when eaten young. At 6–8 inches, young ridged luffa fruits have a mild, delicate flavor similar to mild zucchini with a slightly slippery texture — excellent stir-fried, in curries, soups, and Indian sabzi. It is an extremely productive vine in the Gulf Coast heat, producing continuous fruit through summer. The vine also produces attractive, bright-yellow flowers. If fruits are allowed to mature fully and dry, they produce a natural fiber loofah (the vascular skeleton of the mature fruit) — both varieties produce usable loofahs, but ridged luffa fibers are softer. In Indian, Filipino, and Southeast Asian cuisines, ridged luffa is preferred over smooth for vegetable use.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Cucurbits
Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Luffa

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy soil in full sun; round melon (tinda) is an Indian subcontinental cucurbit popular in Indian, Pakistani, and Bangladeshi cooking — it grows rapidly in hot weather and produces abundantly but must be picked young at 3–4 inches for best texture and flavor.

directions: Direct sow in warm soil in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; harvest fruits at 3–4 inches across before skin hardens. Praecitrullus fistulosus (round melon or tinda) is an Indian subcontinent cucurbit — closely related to but distinct from watermelon — that is an important summer vegetable throughout India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh. The small, round, pale-green, slightly fuzzy fruits (2–4 inches across when harvested young) have a mild, delicate flavor and a slightly spongy, moist texture when cooked; they absorb spices and sauces well, making them excellent for Indian curries (tinda masala), stuffed preparations, and stir-fries. The plant is compact and less vigorous than many cucurbits — it can be grown on a small trellis or allowed to sprawl as a groundcover. The fruits must be picked when young and tender; overripe fruits become pithy and seedy. It grows best in the hot summer conditions of zone 8–12 and is rarely available in North American grocery stores, making home growing especially rewarding for Indian cooking enthusiasts. An essentially unknown vegetable in Western horticulture that deserves wider recognition.
sources: Indian Council of Agricultural Research — Tinda
Purdue University — New Crop Resources — Praecitrullus

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Indian coleus is related to ornamental coleus (Plectranthus scutellarioides) and is an easy-to-grow perennial herb in warm climates, roots are harvested for the compound forskolin.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; root cuttings readily in moist soil or water. Plectranthus barbatus (Indian coleus or forskohlii) is a fragrant-leaved tropical herb closely related to ornamental coleus, native to East Africa and tropical Asia and used extensively in Ayurvedic medicine under the name 'makandi' or 'coleus.' The roots of P. barbatus contain the diterpene compound forskolin, which has been extensively studied for its ability to activate the enzyme adenylate cyclase, increasing cyclic AMP (cAMP) levels in cells — a mechanism that has been researched for effects on weight management, glaucoma, and thyroid conditions. Forskohlii supplements (standardized root extracts) are widely sold in the herbal supplement market. As a garden plant, Indian coleus is an attractive, square-stemmed, aromatic, soft-leaved herb with pale-blue flower spikes in late summer — resembling a coarser-leaved version of its ornamental coleus relatives. It roots readily from stem cuttings and grows vigorously in humid, subtropical conditions. CAUTION: May interact with anticoagulants and medications metabolized by the CYP system; consult healthcare provider before medicinal use.
sources: University of Maryland Medical Center — Coleus Forskohlii

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost or start indoors 6–8 weeks earlier in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; palmarosa is a tropical Indian aromatic grass in the same genus as lemongrass, grown for the essential oil distilled from its fresh leaves — one of the world's most important fragrance grass crops.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in warm soil in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; palmarosa is drought-tolerant once established. Cymbopogon martinii (palmarosa or gingergrass) is a tall, tropical Indian aromatic grass closely related to lemongrass (C. citratus), citronella (C. nardus), and vetiver (Chrysopogon zizanioides) — grown commercially as one of the world's most important perfumery and aromatherapy raw material crops. The leaves and stems distill to a pale-yellow essential oil with a soft, sweet, floral-rose fragrance with undertones of geranium — the compound geraniol (up to 90% of the oil) is responsible for the characteristic rose-like smell. Palmarosa oil is used extensively in perfumery as a low-cost rose substitute or adulterant, in cosmetics, soaps, and aromatherapy (antibacterial, skin-conditioning properties), and as a flavoring in some food products. The plant itself resembles a coarser, more upright lemongrass with narrower leaves and a somewhat less pronounced citrus scent — though the leaves are pleasantly fragrant when crushed. As a garden plant in zone 9–12, palmarosa is ornamental and easy to grow; fresh leaves can be used to scent soaps, sachets, and infused oils at home.
sources: Purdue University — Essential Oil Crops — Cymbopogon martinii
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Aromatic Herbs
International Journal of Essential Oil Therapeutics
